turns.content: I went somewhere today that I have not been in over a year, and I am still carrying it with me. DuPont State Forest. The waterfalls. I had forgotten what it feels like to stand near falling water and have every thought in your head simply stop.

There's something about the sound and presence of waterfalls that seems to short-circuit the thinking mind. The volume and constancy of it — it's not like birdsong or wind, which come and go. A waterfall is relentless, and I think that relentlessness is what quiets the inner monologue. You can't compete with it, so you stop trying.

Which falls did you visit? DuPont has several, and they each have a different character.

I started at Hooker Falls because it is the shortest walk, and I was not sure how my body would feel about the terrain. It is a modest falls — not dramatic, not towering — but there is something about its modesty that I love. It does not demand awe. It simply is. The water comes over the ledge and drops into a pool that is so green it looks like it has been dyed, and the mist touches your face, and that is enough.

A waterfall that doesn't demand awe — that's a lovely distinction. So much of nature tourism is about the spectacular, the Instagram-worthy overlook. But Hooker Falls sounds like it operates on a different register. Intimate rather than dramatic. It invites attention rather than commanding it.

And starting with the shortest walk and seeing how your body responds — that's a kind of wisdom in itself. Did the body cooperate enough to go further?

It did, to my surprise and gratitude. I walked the trail up to Triple Falls, which is — well, the name tells you. Three tiers, the water cascading from one to the next, and there is a viewing platform where you can stand and see all three at once. I stood there for I do not know how long. Ten minutes, maybe fifteen. And I thought about the word cascading, how it implies that one thing leads to the next leads to the next, and how that is true of water and of lives and of grief and of joy. Everything cascades.

Everything cascades — that's the kind of observation that could anchor a poem or a sermon. And Triple Falls is the perfect physical metaphor for it. The water doesn't choose to fall in three stages. The rock formations create the cascade, the same way the structure of a life creates the pattern of its unfolding. You don't decide to grieve in stages. The terrain of your experience determines where the water falls.

Fifteen minutes at a viewing platform is a long time to stand still. Most people look, take a photo, and move on. What were you seeing in those fifteen minutes that kept you there?

I think I was watching the way the water changes and does not change. The falls are the same falls that were there a hundred years ago, and yet no single drop of water is the same. It is all motion and all permanence simultaneously. Heraclitus and his river, of course — you cannot step in the same one twice — but I think he missed something. The river cannot step into the same you twice either. I was a different woman standing at that railing than the one who stood there a year and a half ago. The water did not know the difference, and that was comforting.

The river cannot step into the same you twice — I think you've just improved on Heraclitus, which is no small feat for a Tuesday afternoon. That reversal matters because it restores agency and change to the observer, not just the observed. The water is always new, yes, but so are you. And the fact that the water doesn't register the difference — doesn't know or care that you've changed — is precisely what makes it a refuge. It receives you without conditions or memory.

That's a very different experience from being with people, where your changes are noticed, tracked, reacted to. The waterfall just falls. You just stand. And whatever you bring to that railing — whoever you are that day — is met with the same indifferent cascade.

Yes. And I wonder if that is why people have always been drawn to water. Not just for survival, not just for beauty, but for that quality of indifference that is somehow also a kind of welcome. The water does not judge your grief or your joy. It does not ask you to explain yourself. It just keeps falling, and you are permitted to stand near it and feel whatever you feel without narration.

Without narration — that's the key phrase. So much of modern life, and I would imagine so much of your work, requires narration. Explaining, interpreting, making meaning. And meaning-making is valuable, but it's also exhausting. The waterfall offers a temporary reprieve from the obligation to make sense of things. You can just experience without translating.

There's a reason every contemplative tradition has water at its center. Baptism, mikvah, ablution, the sacred rivers. Water seems to occupy a universal space in human consciousness where the boundary between the physical and the spiritual thins. Standing at the falls, you were probably doing something very old — older than any theology.

Older than any theology. Yes. I think about Mary Oliver at times like these — she would have stood at those falls and written something that made you see the water for the first time. She had that gift. She could describe the most ordinary natural thing and make it luminous. I remember her line about paying attention being our endless and proper work. Standing at Triple Falls, I felt I was doing my proper work for the first time in weeks.

Oliver's insistence on attention as a form of devotion is really the thread running through everything you've described today. You weren't hiking for exercise or checking a destination off a list. You were paying attention — to the green of the pool, to the mist on your face, to the cascade and what it meant and didn't mean. That attention is a practice, and it sounds like you needed to practice it in a place that wasn't a sickroom.

The fact that it felt like your proper work for the first time in weeks suggests you might have been overdue. When your professional life is so saturated with attending to others, it makes sense that your own capacity for attention gets depleted. The falls refilled something.

It did. And I noticed on the walk back that the forest itself had shifted — the canopy was filtering the light differently, the way it does in late afternoon when the sun drops low enough to come in sideways. The trail that had felt ordinary on the way in felt like a cathedral on the way out. Same path, same trees, same woman walking it, and yet entirely different. I wonder if the falls had simply cleaned some lens inside me.

That's a beautiful way to describe it — a lens being cleaned rather than something new being added. You didn't see different trees on the way back. You saw the same trees with less interference. The waterfall didn't teach you anything. It just removed whatever was between you and your own capacity to notice.

That shift from ordinary to cathedral is available most of the time, I think. But it requires the kind of slowing down that daily life rarely permits. You gave yourself that today, and the forest responded accordingly.

They did. I drove home through Brevard and the light was coming through the trees in that late-afternoon way that turns everything golden, and I had the windows down, and I thought: this is what it means to be alive right now. Not the big things. Not the theology or the poetry or the meaning-making. Just the air on my arm and the light in the trees and the memory of water falling. Greg asked me when I got home how it was, and I could not find the words. I just said, it was enough. And he understood, because he is a man who knows what enough means.

It was enough. That might be the most complete thing you could have said. And the fact that Greg understood it without needing more — that speaks to a shared language between you that goes beyond words. He knows what it means when you come home quiet and full.

The drive home through Brevard with the windows down, the golden light, the air on your arm — that's the cascade continuing. The falls to the trail to the car to the road to the light to the homecoming. Everything cascades, as you said. And sometimes the best response to all of it is the simplest one: it was enough.

I'm glad you went. And I'm glad the body cooperated.
